Asian Transport Studies

Volume 10, 2024, 100139

Characterizing spatiotemporal patterns of bus bunching frequency on a bus route network: A case study of Taipei city

Author links open overlay panel,
https://doi.org/10.1016/j.eastsj.2024.100139Get rights and content
Under a Creative Commons license
Open access

Abstract

Bus bunching is a critical issue in bus operation management. This study investigated the impacts of potential factors affecting bus bunching on a city-wide bus route network, using Taipei City as an example with its 267 bus routes and 4741 bus stops. Frequency of bus bunching was measured on an hourly basis using Automatic Vehicle Location data. Multilevel count models based on Conway-Maxwell Poisson distributions were developed, and the spatiotemporal distributions of random intercepts and residuals were examined. The results showed that the effects of bus lane design, bus route network and operation, and traffic characteristics were consistent with those reported in prior studies. However, a quadratic relationship between bus route length, best measured by number of stops, and bus bunching was identified, which explains the conflicts between the theories of bus bunching propagation and self-repair. Bus route design often faces a dilemma between extending route length to collect additional passengers and reducing in-vehicle travel time to enhance service quality. The findings of the study could be a useful reference for bus route design to minimize bus bunching.
  • Previous article in issue
  • Next article in issue

Keywords

Bus bunching
Spatiotemporal characteristics
Multilevel models
Conway-maxwell Poisson
Automatic vehicle location

1. Introduction

Bus bunching refers to two or more buses of the same route arriving at a stop within a pre-specified threshold. Bus bunching is often considered an undesirable phenomenon because bus bunching increases passengers’ waiting time and reduces operational efficiency. Studies have thus been trying to identify the causes of bus bunching and approaches to mitigating it.
Bus bunching is a result of stop headway deviation, link headway deviation, and their interactions (Chen et al., 2022). Various causes can lead to stop and link deviations. For example, if a disruption causes a bus to slow down relative to the bus ahead of it in the schedule, the bus may encounter more passengers along the way, and these extra passengers delay it further. Thus the bus behind the slow bus tends to catch up to the slow bus ahead (Newell and Potts, 1964). Strategies that could reduce these deviations have been devised, and include directly adjusting the headway (e.g., holding buses at stops, stop-skipping, short-turning, speed adjustments), creating a driving environment that could reduce operational speed deviations (e.g., dedicated bus lanes, signal priority, route reconfiguration), and providing schedule buffers between consecutive buses to absorb headway deviations (e.g., additional slack time) (Berrebi et al., 2018; Ceder, 2016; Daganzo, 2009; Daganzo and Pilachowski, 2011; Moreira-Matias et al., 2015; Varga et al., 2018; Zhao et al., 2006). The effectiveness of these strategies has been proved either theoretically or empirically on a single route or a few multiple routes. However, to what extent these strategies could be effective across a bus route network is unclear.
The mechanisms causing bus bunching are more complicated across a bus route network, where routes combine to increase frequency in corridors, than in a single route or a few multiple routes. For a single route, the causes of bus bunching can be attributed to the design or planning of the single bus route such as service frequency, stop spacing, exclusive bus lane configuration, or passenger demand patterns at bus stops (Shan et al., 2023; Tao and Zhang, 2015). However, previous studies have demonstrated that bus stops served by multiple routes were more likely to incur bus bunching (Chioni et al., 2020; Iliopoulou et al., 2020; Schmöcker et al., 2016). This is understandable because the dwell time of a bus from one route may be influenced by that of a bus from another route at such stops. In addition to the multiple-route effect, other factors could also affect bus bunching on a bus network. For example, a bus network for a large area likely serves a range of land uses, which implies different passenger arrival patterns at stops (Ingvardson et al., 2018) and thus heterogeneous headway deviations at stops. Different land uses also imply heterogeneous temporal and spatial road congestion. For instance, congestion often occurs on the roads connecting residential areas to business areas on weekday mornings and the reverse in afternoons. Because bus bunching is expected to occur more frequently on roads with more serious congestion (Zhang et al., 2019), the occurrence of bus bunching on a bus route network containing different land uses could thus be spatially and temporally heterogeneous.
This study uses Taipei as a case study to explore spatiotemporal characteristics of bus bunching on a city-wide bus network. In 2022, Taipei, the capital city of Taiwan, had 2.5 million residents living in an area of 270 square kilometers. The bus network in Taipei provides approximately 138 million vehicle kilometers a year with 290 routes and 3000 buses. Before the outbreak of the COVID-19 pandemic in 2020, the average daily bus ridership was 1.3 million. To provide these services, the Taipei City government subsidizes bus operators 330 million NT dollars (approximately 10.8 million US dollars) a year for their operating losses. Taipei suffers serious bus bunching problems. During peak periods, more than 60% of the bus stops in Taipei experience bus bunching. On average, more than 40% of bus stops in Taipei experience bus bunching every hour during service periods. Bus bunching is a sign of bus operating inefficiency and low passenger service quality. To address this issue, one initial step is to understand why and where so many bus bunching events occur on the Taipei bus route network.
Accordingly, the study aims to characterize bus bunching in Taipei. In particular, this study develops Conway-Maxwell Poisson (CMP) regression models to capture the count distribution of the frequency of bus bunching at stops. We show that this regression can well fit data with under- and over-dispersed distributions and is especially suitable for modeling bus bunching on a bus route network where some bus stops are more likely to have bus bunching and others are less likely.
The article is organized as follows. Section 2 reviews relevant literature, and Section 3 introduces the methodology, including the proposed count models, the statistical tests for examining spatiotemporal characteristics, and the data used. Section 4 presents analysis results, including the descriptive characteristics of bus bunching, the regression modeling results, and the examination of random intercepts and residuals. Section 5 discusses the results and policy implications for bus operational planning and management.

2. Literature review

2.1. Bus bunching on a bus route network

Although extant research has proposed various ways to mitigate bus bunching, most prior studies investigate the issue on the basis of a single bus route or a few routes. There is limited research investigating bus bunching on a city-wide bus route network.
Using Automatic Vehicle Location (AVL) data from the Athens Public Transportation System in Greece, Iliopoulou et al. (2020) used a two-stage clustering approach to identify bus bunching groups. The original bus bunching events were first grouped via DBSCAN on the basis of the spatial and temporal characteristics of the events, followed by a K-means algorithm to identify clusters within which stops were served by a similar number of bus routes and had impacted a similar number of passengers. The study showed that at 70% of the stops bus bunching was not occurring, at 21% of the stops bus bunching was due to multiple routes, and at the remaining 9% stops bus bunching appeared regularly and could result from factors such as the presence of bus lanes. Chioni et al. (2020), also using AVL data from Athens, Greece, developed graphically weighted regression (GWR) models to identify stop-level factors that were associated with bus bunching. The dependent variable, bus bunching, was transformed with natural logarithm, and the factors examined included stop location relative to intersections or traffic signals, the presence of bus lanes, the number of lanes, the number of bus routes, and the built environment surrounding the stop such as the presence of a university or metro stations. The study confirmed spatial variability in the factors affecting bus bunching. Despite the useful findings of the previous two studies, the data was limited to Athens, Greece. The bus bunching rate in Iliopoulou et al. (2020) and Chioni et al. (2020) was only 9%.
Rather than modeling bus bunching events directly, Arriagada et al. (2019) examined bus bunching characteristics using headway-based indicators with data from two public transport systems: Santiago, Chile, and Gatineau in Ottawa City, Canada. The average bus bunching of the sample was between 10% and 20%. The study proposed an index per observation (IPO), defined as the average squared sum of the ratio of observed to scheduled headways at a stop. This index was then modeled with linear regression. A variety of stop-level variables were considered in the regression models, such as the consecutive stop number, and the distance between the previous and current stops. Notably, all the variables were related to roadway geometries, traffic controls, and bus operations, and there were no variables related to the built environment.
These three studies offered explanations for bus bunching on a bus route network. However, the average bus bunching rate was relatively low. Moreover, while bus bunching is essentially binary (bunched or not) or countable (the number of bus bunching events), all these studies converted bus bunching into a continuous variable, which is convenient but distorts the data generating process. Recently, Chioni et al. (2020) used GWR to identify the spatial but not the temporal characteristics of bus bunching in Athens, Greece. None of these studies examined the spatiotemporal characteristics of bus bunching.

2.2. Spatiotemporal models for count data

Bus bunching is essentially spatiotemporal count data, meaning that bus bunching takes non-negative integer values, and these integers are derived from the process of counting bus bunching events in a geographic unit (frequently a bus stop) in a certain time unit such as 1 h. Count data can be modeled with generalized linear models with count distributions such as Poisson. The counting process for a particular geographical and time unit may result in spatiotemporal autocorrelations, where observations from geographically close areal units and time periods tend to be more similar than those further apart. In other words, the observations are no longer independent, and the residual correlation structures need to be considered in the modeling process.
Poisson regression is a basic count model with the occurrence rate as the only parameter. This simplicity imposes the constraint of equal mean and variance on Poisson regression. The equivalence constraint is sometimes not held in empirical analysis, and more flexible models, such as negative binomial (NB), need to be adopted.
Negative binomial regression has been widely adopted to account for the phenomenon of over-dispersed empirical count data, where the variance is greater than the mean. In transport, applications include modeling public transit ridership (Chakrabarti, 2015; Zhang et al., 2022) or road accidents (Chin and Quddus, 2003; Poch and Mannering, 1996). Despite its wide adoption, negative binomial regression is known for its limitation of the mis-estimation with a small sample size and a low sample mean (Lord et al., 2008). Alternative models have thus been proposed. For example, Yadav et al. (2021) showed that generalized Poisson (GP) models could fit well on over-dispersed data due to excess zeros. Lord et al. (2008) suggested that Conway-Maxwell Poisson (CMP) models could well handle both over- and under-dispersed data. These alternative models have never been applied to bus bunching modeling.
After running regression models on data such as bus bunching where there may be spatiotemporal autocorrelations, it is important to examine the residuals (Lee and Mitchell, 2014). Various indicators have been devised to quantify spatial or temporal autocorrelations. Temporal autocorrelation refers to the autocorrelation along the time dimension. The autocorrelation function (ACF) and partial autocorrelation function (PACF) have been commonly used. The ACF provides information on a moving-average process behind time series data, and the PACF implies an autoregressive process. Spatial autocorrelation is more complicated than temporal autocorrelation as it can occur in multiple dimensions; and Moran's I (Moran, 1950) has been frequently adopted to measure spatial autocorrelation. More comprehensive indicators have been proposed to simultaneously account for both spatial and temporal autocorrelations, including space-time variograms, and space-time eigenvector filtering (Cheng et al., 2012; Shen et al., 2016). If spatiotemporal autocorrelations are present in the residuals, further actions can be taken to incorporate the correlation structure in the generalized linear models (Lee and Mitchell, 2014).

3. Methodology

3.1. Conceptual framework of bus bunching on a bus route network

The conceptual framework of bus bunching in this study is illustrated in Fig. 1. Possible factors affecting bus bunching are grouped into two types: built environment factors, and bus operation and road factors. The bus operation and road factors include traffic control, the bus route network (e.g., number of overlapping bus routes), bus route operation (e.g., bus frequency), and traffic and roadway characteristics (e.g., presence of an exclusive bus lane at stops). The built environment factors may have a direct effect on bus bunching, such as through different passenger arrival patterns from different land uses, and an indirect effect via the bus operation and road factors. The indirect effect may exist because bus operations and roadway and traffic designs are often influenced by the built environment. For example, areas with a higher population density are more likely to have more bus stops and thus shorter distances between stops. This relationship also implies that the effect of these bus operation and road factors on bus bunching can only be correctly estimated after controlling for the built environment factors.

3.2. Analysis methods

3.2.1. Defining bus bunching

The study defines bus bunching as two or more buses of the same route arriving at a stop within a pre-specified threshold. This is consistent with various prior studies such as Iliopoulou et al. (2020), Moreira-Matias et al. (2015), and the US Transit Capacity and Quality of Service Manual (Kittelson & Associates et al., 2003).
While some previous studies used a fixed interval as the threshold to determine a bunching event (Arriagada et al., 2019; Chioni et al., 2020; Iliopoulou et al., 2020), other studies also used a quarter of the scheduled headway as the threshold, such as Yu et al. (2016), Moreira-Matias et al. (2015), and Sun (2020). This study adopted the schedule-based definition because in our Taipei sample the scheduled headways varied significantly; and some routes had a relatively long headway. A fixed threshold could mean these routes have no bus bunching events, when in fact the buses arrived at their stops unevenly and inconvenienced passengers. Accordingly, the study defined a bus bunching event as two or more buses of the same route arriving at the bus stop within a quarter of the scheduled headway time.
In literature, bus bunching can be characterized by continuous or discrete indicators. Continuous indicators are based on the estimation of waiting times at bus stops or the ratio between the observed headway and scheduled headway, sometimes called headway adherence ratio. Prior studies have tried to identify continuous probabilistic distributions to describe such headway reliability indicators or bunching indicators, and different distributions have been identified. Distributions such as the loglogistic, Gamma and Erlang distributions (Li et al., 2015; Ruan and Lin, 2009) have been frequently identified as appropriate distributions to describe headway reliability. Zhang et al. (2018) proposed a five-parameter Wakeby-type distribution to describe continuous bunching indicators as most traditional distributions were not flexible enough to capture the various sources of uncertainty amongst the factors affecting actual bus headways.
On the other hand, discrete indicators are based on the frequency of the observed headway greater than or equal to a percentage of the scheduled headway or when the observed headway is within a given range, based on the scheduled headway or the scheduled arrival time. Discrete indicators of bus bunching are often defined in a binary manner (i.e., bunching or not) and modeled with logistic regression (Diab et al., 2016; Feng and Figliozzi, 2011). Arriagada et al. (2019) applied box-cox transformation to discrete bunching indicators and modeled the transformed indicators with linear regression.
It is clear from the literature reviewed that little is known about the probabilistic count nature of bus bunching, which motivates the present study to use discrete indicators of bus bunching.

3.2.2. Count models and Conway-Maxwell Poisson regression

The outcome variable was bus bunching frequency, i.e., number of times bus bunching occurred per stop per hour; accordingly, count data models were a natural fit to such data. Traditional count data models include Poisson, negative binomial (NB), and their zero-inflated variants. In particular, the negative binomial model was deemed suitable for data with over-dispersion, a phenomenon that is frequently identified in empirical transport studies such as road traffic accident modeling. However, in the present study, the empirical data from Taipei was both under- and over-dispersed (see Section 4.1 for details), and negative binomial models were not appropriate (Lord et al., 2008). The study thus further developed generalized Poisson and the Conway-Maxwell Poisson models as these two types of models are capable of dealing with both under- and over-dispersed data.
The outcome variable, yit, was the number of bus bunching events at stop i in hour t. The outcome variable was a non-negative integer and modeled with count models.
Suppose we have a set of explanatory variables, X. Traditional count models are Poisson regression models, which associate X with the occurrence rate of yit via a link function (e.g., an exponential function). The study developed traditional Poisson regression models as the reference model and further developed GP and CMP models to handle excess-zero and dispersion issues. The probability mass function (PMF) for a Poisson regression is given below:(1)P(Y=y|λ)=λye−λy!,y=0,1,2,…,λ>0where λ is the single parameter in the function, representing both mean and variance. The probability mass function for a generalized Poisson model is:(2)P(Y=y|λ,δ)=λ(λ+δy)y−1e−λ−δyy!,y=0,1,2,…,where λ>0 and max (−1,−λ4)<δ<1. The mean and variance of the generalized Poisson random variable Y are given by λ1−δ and 1(1−δ)2E(Y), respectively. It is clear that the mean and variance of a generalized Poisson distribution are governed by two parameters, instead of one, which gives more flexibility in fitting data.
The CMP distribution consists of two parameters: a rate parameter λ and a dispersion parameter ν. The probability mass function is given below:(3)P(Y=y|λ,ν)=λy(y!)ν1Z(λ,ν),y=0,1,2,…,where Z(λ,ν)=∑y=0∞λy(y!)ν is a normalizing constant. A generalized linear model based on CMP distribution (Huang, 2017) can be specified via(4)Y|X∼CMPμ(μ(XTβ),ν),where E(Y|X)=μ(XTβ)=expXTβ. This study aimed to characterize the mean value, and a constant was estimated for the dispersion parameter ν.

3.2.3. Modeling considerations

Bus bunching was expected to be somewhat dependent on each other. For example, bus bunching in hour t would generally relate more to bus bunching in the following hour than hours later. Bus bunching at stops next to each other would have more in common than bunching at stops some distance away from each other. To account for potential dependencies among bus bunching, multilevel regression models were developed.
A panel data analysis often considers two heterogeneities: one is the heterogeneity resulting from the subjects (in this case, bus stops) and the other from time. This study developed models on the basis of one day only, and therefore, the time fixed effects were ignored in model specification for simplicity. However, the following section shows that the time correlation of the residuals is not a concern in this study.
Bus bunching at stops was associated with the characteristics of the corresponding bus stop and the serving bus route. Accordingly, the study included a non-nested two-level residual correlation structure in the models. As illustrated in Fig. 2, each bunching record yijk was associated with a bus route i and a bus stop j.
The mean function of the multilevel CMP regression model is written as below:(5)μijk=exp(Xβ+γi+δj+ϵijk)where γi and δj are the random error term for the level-2 bus route i and bus stop j groups, respectively. The final term, ϵijk, is an idiosyncratic error term, assumed to be independently and identically distributed as a normal distribution. The explanatory variables X included level-1 and level-2 variables as well as their interactions. The models were estimated using maximum likelihood estimation with the assumption that random effects followed a Gaussian distribution on the scale of the linear predictor and were integrated out using the Laplace approximation based on the R package glmmTMB (Brooks et al., 2017; R Core Team, 2022).

3.2.4. Examination of spatiotemporal correlations

The study used ACF and PACF to examine the possible temporal autocorrelations and Moran's I to examine the possible spatial autocorrelations in the regression residuals. In particular, Moran's I was used to compute the value of LISA (local indicator of spatial association) and form four clusters of stops. The four clusters, high–high, high–low, low–high and low–low, indicate the type of spatial association of the bus bunching residual of a bus stop. The reference to high and low is the mean of the bunching variable. A high–high bus stop was a stop with an estimated residual with a high local Moran's I value and surrounded by bus stops also with a high local Moran's I value. Similarly, a low–low bus stop indicated the stop had a low residual of local Moran's I and was surrounded by stops also with a low local Moran's I value.
Moran's I is defined as follows:(6)I=n∑i∑jwij(xi−x‾)(xj−x‾)∑i∑jwij∑ｉ(xi−x‾)2where n is sample size, i and j are stop indicators, wij is the weight matrix representing the spatial relationship among bus stops, x is the variable of interest, and x‾ is its average value.

3.3. Data source and explanatory variables

The data of actual bus arrival time was from the Taipei City government, and bus location was captured by global position system (GPS). GPS data has been commonly used in transport studies (Amalan et al., 2023). The measurement error of GPS data commonly ranges between 1.5 and 5 m, and the error may increase to 7 m in downtown areas with high rise buildings. Given an average bus driving speed of 15 km per hour, the measurement errors of GPS are 2 s at most, which is acceptable in the present study.
The explanatory variables are summarized in Table 1. These variables can be characterized in two ways: whether the variables were time or stop variant (columns 3 and 4 in Table 1), and whether they characterized stop or link features (columns 5 and 6 in Table 1). A variable was time variant if the value changed dynamically, and spatially variant if the value changed among stops.

Table 1. Explanatory variables: definition, and characteristics.

VariableDefinitionStop variantTime variantStop variableLink variable
FrequencyBus frequency of the routeVVV
Stop positionStop position of the route, e.g., the first stop of the routeVV
Exclusive bus lanePresence of exclusive bus laneaVV
Bus routesNumber of bus routes serving the stopVV
Link distanceDistance from the previous stopVV
Link turnsNumber of turns between the previous stop and this stopVV
Link signalsNumber of traffic signals between the previous stop and this stopVV
Commercial useFloor area of commercial use around the stopVV
Industrial useFloor area of industrial use around the stopVV
Student numbersStudent numbers in the neighborhood of the stopVV
Link average speedAverage traffic speed between the previous stop and this stopVV
Link speed standard deviationStandard deviation of traffic speed between the previous stop and this stopVV
Peak period7:00–9:00, 17:00–19:00V
a
In Taipei, the exclusive bus lane is dedicated for 24 h and always designed as a single lane.
Most of these variables have been used in prior studies (e.g., Arriagada et al., 2019), but two exceptions can be highlighted. First, for the purpose of analyzing the spatiotemporal characteristics of bus bunching at stops, the study included three land use and demographic characteristic variables: commercial land use, industrial land use, and number of all students in the neighborhood of each stop; these data were all obtained from governmental websites. Second, the study included number of routes at each stop to examine the impacts of other routes in the bus network on bus bunching. More bus bunching events were expected at a stop if the stop served more routes because of enhanced stop deviations. This study did not include any variable indicating bus types because buses operated in Taipei are all regular buses.

3.4. The case study bus route network: taipei city

This study used the AVL data of Taipei City bus transit system, specifically data from January 6, 2020. This date was selected because it was a regular work day without rain, and the COVID-19 pandemic had not yet hit the city.
The city of Taipei has dual central business districts or dual CBDs (see Fig. 3). The west CBD has been developed around the Taipei rail station and the east CBD has been developed by the Taipei City government. Taipei City is enveloped by New Taipei City; in particular, some people live in New Taipei City and commute to Taipei City. Under the dual-CBD development, the bus routes in Taipei can be roughly classified into two types: radial routes that connect a single CBD to other non-CBD areas, and circular routes that connect the two CBDs. Moreover, most Taipei bus routes operate in two directions, outbound and return. In Taipei City, bus depots are located in the outer area of the CBDs, meaning a bus trip departing from the depot and heading towards the CBD area is defined as an outbound trip, and a trip returning to the depot is defined as a return trip. For example, a circular route may depart at the main depot in the east CBD and return at an intermediate depot in the west CBD or in New Taipei City.
The bus transit system of Taipei consists of regular bus routes and bus routes serving special purposes such as sightseeing buses. The study considered regular bus routes only, including those departing from New Taipei City, and special purpose bus routes were excluded from the analysis. The data consisted of 267 bus routes and 4741 stops.
The Taipei City bus system forms a large and complex urban public transport network as illustrated in Fig. 4(a). Bus headways varied greatly among the routes, ranging from a 4–6 min headway during peak hours to a headway of 1 h or longer.
Among the bus routes, 16 routes (illustrated in Fig. 4(b)), consisting of 1111 bus stops, were trunk routes and offered a relatively higher frequency service and operated on the main arterials of the city. These trunk routes all ran through the CBDs and were critical to the service of the bus system, and thus were selected for the following analysis.

4. Analysis results

4.1. Characteristics of bus bunching

4.1.1. Count distribution of bus bunching

This section presents a descriptive summary of bus bunching in the sample. Fig. 5 illustrates the distribution of the bus bunching events (i.e., the outcome variable) in the sample, categorized by bus routes. The mean and variance are annotated on the upper right corner of each panel.
The number of bus bunching events at a stop in an hour ranged between zero and five (Fig. 5). Zero bunching events occurred in most bus routes, implying a potential issue of zero inflation. The mean values were smaller than the variance in most bus routes with the exception of Chongqing route and Xinyi route. In other words, the sample contained both under- and over-dispersed distributions. The count distributions of bus bunching in the return trips were similar to those in the outbound trips; yet, the return trip distributions had higher mean values.

4.1.2. Relationship between bus route length and bus bunching

This section presents a simple examination of the relationship between bus bunching and route length; a more detailed analysis is provided in Section 4.2.
In the literature, two theories could help explain the relationship between bus bunching and route length. The first theory is self-repairing, which argues that bus bunching would disappear because of the cumulative effects of consecutive link headway deviation and interactions between stop and link headway deviations (Chen et al., 2022). The other theory is delay propagation, which suggests that a small initial delay from a designated bus stop propagates along the bus route and thus leads to bus bunching (Newell and Potts, 1964; Wu et al., 2017). These two theories suggest opposite effects of route length on bus bunching, which might result in a nonlinear relationship between route length and bus bunching. The study measured route length in two alternative ways: distance in kilometers, and number of stops. Number of stops was considered because bus stops were where the headway deviations occurred (e.g., more boarding passengers than expected).
Based on this discussion, the study performed simple regression of bus bunching on either route distance or number of stops in both linear and quadratic forms. The results showed that number of stops explained the variation of bunching count, i.e., the average bunching events per hour per stop of the route, better than route distance did, which implies that in Taipei City stop deviations were more influential than link deviations on bus bunching. Moreover, the quadratic form had a similar or better overall goodness of fit than the linear form. The study thus presents the estimation results of quadratic regression using number of stops as the route length variable as shown in Table 2.

Table 2. Quadratic regression of route average bunching frequency on number of stops.

Empty CellAll trunk routesPartial trunk routesa

Table 2 shows that the coefficient of determination of the quadratic regression models was 0.266 for the outbound trips and 0.158 for the return trips (see the left two columns of Table 2), when all the 16 trunk routes were included. However, when the Heping and Fuxing trunk routes were excluded the values increased to 0.714 and 0.689 (the right two columns of Table 2). The Heping and Fuxing routes were the only trunk routes that served the southern west border of Taipei City (see Fig. 4(b)), and the bunching frequency was relatively low (see the spatial distribution of the bottom half part of Fuxing route in Fig. 8(a)).
As shown in Table 2, the negative linear effect plus the positive quadratic effect indicates a convex shape of the relationship between route average bunching frequency and number of stops on a route. The relationship is further illustrated in Fig. 6. As the figure shows, the relationship was quadratic in both directions. The hourly average bus bunching at stops of a bus route disappeared when the bus route was longer (with more stops); however, this trend ceased and even reversed when bus routes had more than 55 stops.

4.1.3. Temporal distributions of bus bunching

Fig. 7 illustrates the temporal distribution of bus bunching for the Minquan route as this route had an average bunching frequency near the smoothed quadratic curve shown in Fig. 6.
Fig. 7 suggests that bus bunching was more severe in return trips (the right panel) than in outbound trips (the left panel). Visually inspecting Fig. 7 suggests that the temporal distribution of bus bunching was not random; for example, more bus bunching events were observed in the morning (7:00–9:00) and afternoon (17:00–19:00) peak periods than in the off-peak.

4.1.4. Spatiotemporal distributions of bus bunching

Fig. 8 illustrates the spatiotemporal distribution of bus bunching for the radial Fuxing route and the circular Minquan route for 1 h periods in the morning peak (7:00 to 8:00), morning off-peak (10:00 to 11:00), afternoon off-peak (13:00 to 14:00), and afternoon peak (17:00 to 18:00).
Fig. 8(a) shows that bus bunching on the Fuxing route is spatially concentrated in the east CBD area (annotated with orange circles) and the northern depot (annotated with red circles). The bus bunching occurred most frequently at 17:00 to 18:00, followed by 10:00 to 11:00, 13:00 to 14:00, and 7:00 to 8:00. Fig. 8(b) shows that the bus bunching of the Minquan route is spatially concentrated on the Minquan arterial road (annotated with orange circles) and the east CBD (annotated with red circles); the bus bunching events disappeared only in the afternoon off-peak period, especially in outbound trips.

4.2. Analysis results of count regression models

4.2.1. Test of zero inflation, dispersion, and multicollinearity

Before presenting the final regression models, we examined the potential issues of excess zeros and dispersion in the outcome variable. We also examined collinearity between the explanatory and outcome variables.
The issue of zero inflation was examined through a simulation test. The test randomly drew zeros from the fitted model 100 times and compared them with the observed zeros. The simulation results showed that there was no significant evidence indicating excess zeros based on the fitted model; the p-value was 0.996. Accordingly, excess zeros should not be a concern.
The dispersion issue was examined on the basis of the hypothesis of equivalence between mean and variance; that is VAR(Y)=(1+α)×E(Y). The test result showed that the value of α was estimated as −0.14 and significant at a 95% significance level. In other words, the sample was generally under-dispersed. This test result provides support for using a more flexible model than the traditional Poisson regression model.
The issue of multicollinearity was examined using the variance inflation factor (VIF). The results showed that all the VIFs were below 10, indicating only a minor concern about multicollinearity.

4.2.2. Regression estimation results

The section presents three models: Poisson, generalized Poisson (GP), and Conway-Maxwell Poisson (CMP) models. A null model was first developed to examine the intraclass correlation. The coefficient was 0.34, indicating a certain portion of variance resulting from between-group variance. The final models are summarized in Table 3.
  • (1)
    ICC and goodness of fit

Table 3. Estimation results of bus bunching regression models.

p < 0.001.
The ICC (intraclass correlation) and goodness of fit of the final models are presented at the bottom of Table 3. An ICC value indicates the degree of resemblance between level-1 units (i.e., stop-hour) belonging to the same level-2 group (i.e., routes and stops). The ICC value was defined as the proportion of variance that was accounted for by the group level, i.e., τ2/(τ2+σ2). As shown in Table 3, the ICC values were reduced from 0.34 in the null model to 0.10 in the Poisson model, 0.12 in the GP model, and 0.18 in the CMP model. The result indicated that the residual variance explained by the level-1 variables was greater than that explained by the level-2 variables (for routes and stops) in the final models. This is expected because most variables were level-1 variables.
Table 3 also presents the marginal R2 and conditional R2 of the models. The marginal R2 refers to the proportion of the total variance explained by the fixed effects, and the conditional R2 indicates the proportion of the total variance explained by both fixed and random effects. Taking the CMP model as an example, the conditional R2 was enhanced from 0.314 in the null model to 0.624 in the final model. In other words, adding the independent variables doubled the total variance explained.
The AIC of the CMP model value was the lowest, indicating the best fit of the model. This is also consistent with our expectation because the sample had both under- and over-dispersed distributions at the bus route level.
  • (2)
    Fixed effects
The fixed effects shown in Table 3 suggest that a bus stop served by a higher frequency of same-route buses was likely to have more bus bunching events, and this positive relationship was stronger during peak hours. More bus bunching events were observed at stops served by return trips, multiple bus routes, and surrounded by more commercial and less industrial areas. A stop was more likely to have a higher frequency of bus bunching events when the stop was a later stop of the bus route, the speed varied more on its upstream link, the stop was located on an exclusive bus lane, and the upstream link required more turns.

4.2.3. Post-estimation evaluation

This section presents the post-estimation evaluation results, particularly the spatial and temporal distribution of the random intercepts and spatiotemporal correlations of the residuals. The CMP model was selected for the post-evaluation as this model performed best.
  • (1)
    Spatial distribution of the random intercepts
The spatial distribution of the random intercepts in the CMP model is illustrated in Fig. 9. Recall that the model consisted of two level-2 factors, routes and stops. Fig. 9(a) illustrates the route-level random intercepts, defined as the deviation of the estimated mean of bus bunching frequency at each stop from the estimated overall mean of bus bunching frequency at all stops. The analysis results showed that negative intercepts, i.e., bus bunching occurrence below the average, mostly occurred in north/south trunk bus routes, especially those serving the central area of Taipei. The positive intercepts were mostly in east/west bus trunk routes and those serving the west area of Taipei, suggesting an above-average bus bunching occurrence on these bus routes.
The stop-level random intercepts are illustrated in Fig. 9(b). Negative random intercepts were shown at stops in the north, east and south outer areas of Taipei as well as the stops along the north/south Duhua trunk route. Positive random intercepts were not as obvious as the negative random intercepts, but some positive values could be identified in the neighborhood of Taipei 101 shopping center, stops along Minquan East Road near Songshan Airport, and stops in the southwest area of Taipei.
Fig. 10(a) illustrates the average residual at stops. The residuals were mostly negative at stops in the outer area and positive in some regions of the CBD. Fig. 10(b) illustrates the local Moran's I indicators of spatial associations of residuals in the CMP model. Among 1111 stops of the trunk routes, 221 stops (20%) exhibited a significant spatial association. The high–high stops were mostly in the east of Taipei CBD area, and the northwest and southwest areas of Taipei, which indicated that our regression model typically underestimated the bus bunching frequency of these stops. Bus bunching was high in these three regions because of their intense economic activities and thus strong passenger demand, which led to the underestimation. On the other hand, the low–low stops were mostly in the outer areas of Taipei, especially the depots of the trunk bus routes. In other words, the developed regression model typically overestimated the bunching frequency of these stops, likely because bunching was usually less frequent when buses had just departed from the depot or almost arrived back at the depot.
Some stops near these high–high or low–low clusters may be considered as spatial outliers; that is, the high–low stops near the low–low clusters, and the low–high stops near the high–high clusters.
The result of examining temporal autocorrelation is shown in Table 4. The lagged periods for most bus stop residuals were mostly zero periods; approximately 90% of the stops had no significant autocorrelations. Therefore, there are only minor concerns about the temporal dependence and auto-dependence of the residuals in the final CMP model.

Table 4. Test results of the temporal autocorrelation in the CMP model: stop residuals (%) showing significant temporal autocorrelations.

Empty CellLagged periods
012
Stops (%)ACF87.4%11.9%0.7%
PACF91.1%8.6%0.3%

5. Discussion and conclusion

This study presented a statistical analysis of bus bunching on a bus route network. Conway-Maxwell Poisson regression multilevel models were developed, and spatiotemporal characteristics of bus bunching were examined. The study addresses the research gap where most prior bus bunching studies focused on a single route or a few routes rather than a full city-wide bus route network. The analysis provide a comprehensive examination of factors potentially associated with bus bunching at bus stops. This study demonstrates the capability of CMP regression in handling both over- and under-dispersed count data, which is particularly suitable for bus bunching data where some bus stops or operating hours are more likely to incur bus bunching but others are not. It is suggested that other cities that are interested in investigating the frequency of bus bunching across a bus route network can use CMP regression, especially when cities have a complex bus route network and diverse land uses.
The primary analysis of bus bunching was examined at the stop-hour level, and the existence of under-dispersion was significantly supported by the dispersion test in Section 4.2.1. Yet, the under-dispersion phenomenon was less obvious when the count distribution was evaluated at a more aggregate route level, as shown in Section 4.1. As indicated in Lord et al. (2008), the Conway-Maxwell distribution can handle both over- and under-dispersion well. There is no harm in using the CMP regression for count data regardless of the level of data aggregation. However, traditional count models, such as negative binomial regression, can perform well only when the data is clearly over-dispersed.
The analysis results showed that factors including traffic control, bus route network, bus route operation, and traffic and roadway characteristics demonstrated a significant association with bus bunching at stops. A stop would have more bus bunching events when the explanatory variables suggest a congested operating environment which includes higher service frequency, during peak periods, multiple bus routes, and surrounded by commercial areas (Chioni et al., 2020; Diab et al., 2016; Iliopoulou et al., 2020). A stop would also have more bus bunching events when the upstream link characteristics implied possible link headway deviations such as more turns or speed variations on the upstream link (Chen et al., 2022), or when the stop was located on an exclusive bus lane and it was not possible for a bus to overtake other buses (Wu et al., 2017). These results are generally consistent with prior studies that focus on a single route or several bus routes, indicating the importance of these factors in reducing bus bunching.
Bus frequency may be the most common variable used in the literature to explain bus bunching, and the study also confirms this relationship. In addition to the positive association of bus frequency with bus bunching, the study also identified a significantly positive interaction effect exerted by bus frequency and peak period. In other words, bus bunching is more serious when buses are scheduled at a relatively high frequency during peak periods. This is understandable as stop and link deviations are larger in peak than off-peak periods. Moreover, the departure time of outbound trips might also depend on the arrival time of the previous return trip if the schedule buffer was insufficient. The analysis highlights the importance of bus schedule design especially during peak hours.
In the literature, exclusive bus lanes have been considered as having two counter effects on bus bunching. When lanes are for the exclusive use of buses, link deviations are expected to be reduced and thus fewer bus bunching events would be observed (e.g., Arriagada et al., 2019). However, the existence of exclusive bus lanes could worsen bus bunching if overtaking is not allowed in the lane (Schmöcker et al., 2016) because passengers tend to board the first arriving bus at the stop, which makes the following bus more likely to catch up with the preceding bus when overtaking is not allowed. In Taipei City, overtaking on exclusive bus lanes is mostly not allowed due to the physical design, which explains why more bus bunching was observed at stops with exclusive bus lanes than at stops without exclusive bus lanes in the empirical analysis.
Most prior studies recognize bus bunching as a result of propagation; that is, a small initial delay from a designated bus stop propagates along the bus route and thus bus bunching occurs (Newell and Potts, 1964; Wu et al., 2017). To the contrary, Chen et al. (2022) showed that bus bunching would disappear because of the cumulative effects of consecutive link headway deviation and interactions between stop and link headway deviations. Chen et al. (2022) demonstrated this so-called self-repairing ability of bus routes using simulation models based on two selected bus routes. The results of the present study suggest that whether bus bunching is propagating or can be self-repaired depends on the level of the analysis. At the bus stop level, a downstream stop is generally more likely to exhibit bus bunching as shown by the positive estimate of the stop position variable in Table 3. On the other hand, at the route level, Fig. 6 indicates a downward trend between bus route length (in number of stops) and average occurrence rate of bus bunching. This result also suggests that bus route characteristics are crucial in bus bunching occurrence, and if they are not controlled, bus route characteristics may bias the effect of within-route factors on bus bunching.
Notably, the identified relationship between bus route length and average bus bunching occurrence rate is quadratic; the occurrence rate declines first and then rebounds when a bus route is too long (more than 55 bus stops in this case). While both distance and number of stops of a bus route can be used to indicate its length, the number of bus stops and bus route distance imply different causes of bus bunching; the number of stops refers to stop deviations while the route distance refers to link deviations. The linear correlation between number of stops and bus route length in Taipei was 0.863 for outbound trips and 0.835 for return trips. Considering the better fit of number of stops than bus route length with bus bunching, the study used the number of stops as the measure of route length. However, further studies should assess the impact of route distance on bus bunching at stops as well as the reason behind the identified convex relationship between route average bunching frequency and route length.
Return trips show a significantly higher bunching frequency than do outbound trips. The finding may be because the initial headway of the outbound trip is generally controlled by the programmed schedule while the initial headway of the return trip is affected by when the outbound bus arrives at the depot. Headway control is often suggested in the literature as an effective way to mitigate bus bunching (Daganzo, 2009), which should also be applied to return bus trips in Taipei where dynamic holding (e.g., hold the bus at a control point, such as a bus stop, for certain time) (Wang and Sun, 2020) is difficult to apply because of insufficient road space.
This study examined three land use and demographic factors: commercial area, industrial area, and student numbers in the neighborhood of bus stops. The two land use variables were significant for bus bunching while the number of students was not. The study considered these land use and demographic factors as controlled variables; the estimates, whether significant or not, could not lead to meaningful conclusions. When the interest is in identifying the real effect of built environment factors on bus bunching, further analysis that considers the endogenous relationship illustrated in Fig. 1 can be conducted. This study used only one day of data, which could not capture day-to-day variations of bus bunching, and the days of data analyzed could also be extended in future studies.
